channel.title: Comments for page "Log of Unexplained Locations"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Log of Unexplained Locations"

item.title: Staff Post - MAST (Collab Pages) - Open
item.content:encoded:
The following logs have been pruned from the log:

+ Pruned logs
– hide block

Unexplained Location UE-655588
Description: A small communal, Christian church marked in city records as "the West Tennesse Peoples Church of Churchism", shown to be entirely constructed of organic matter. The only other feature of note surrounding UE-655588 is that the object was formerly Pastor Henry Ecclesia, who slowly transformed into the church between 1970 and 1971.
Date of Containment: 1971-03-12
Location: Outside Clarksville, Tennesse.
Security Protocol: The church is to be patrolled by two guards, under the cover story of Asbestos Cleanup operations. All individuals who enter the church are to be removed. Any individuals who discover the objects anomalous properties are to be provided with Class-A amnestics.
Note: A small plaque was found engraved on the southwestern flank of the structure, transcribed below:

TO THE GLORY OF GOD 1971:

THIS CHURCH WAS MADE OF AND BY PASTOR HENRY ECCLESIA. HIS SACRIFICE TO BECOME ONE WITH THE LORD AND HIS FOLLOWERS WILL NEVER BE FORGOTTEN. HIS BLOOD, SWEAT, TEARS, ORGANS, SKIN, AND MUSCLES WERE USED TO BUILD A HOME FOR OUR LORD AND HIS SUN. [sic]

”Those who wish to be a church can be under God, for that is his way.” - Jesus Christ (Assumed).

Unexplained Location UE-96843
Description: A small Victorian Era house originally owned by 'Sir Oswald Francis', a tea-trader from the United Kingdom. When the attic door is opened, it will lead to an extradimensional location roughly resembling a massive tea plantation. In the centre of the plantation is a large factory complex. Every individual who enters the pocket dimension will simultaneously appear in both the attic entrance (which leads to a small shack) and the factory. They will also feel an impossible compulsion to harvest tea from the plantation and put the leaves on a pully within the structure, and then to place a weight on the other pully. The individual who moves out of the factory will be unable to exit the dimension, whereas the individuals who enter via the attic entrance will be able to exit. Several notable individuals are within the structure including, but not limited to, George Washington, Winston Churchill, Queen Elizabeth II, Sir Oswald Francis, Kristina Francis, every member of the beetles, and 83 duplicates of an unidentified worker.
Date of Containment: Since Foundation Inception.
Location: Ganges Delta, Bangladesh.
Security Protocol: Two guards stationed around the structure. Produced tea checked for anomalies and sold as a part of Sir Conrod Products (an SCP foundation front company.)

Unexplained Location UE-39221
Description: Roughly 204 billion exoplanets 189 billion light-years from Earth, with several anomalous effects. The planets exist within the radius of each other where gravitational action would result in their destruction, and move in erratic and impossible patterns. The planets, when viewed through a satellite, resemble 21 tangled double-helix structures forming into large structures. Despite these structures large mass, no gravitational effects occur on nearby planets. The double helix structure of this planetary system is sometimes threaded through large masses of planets, which somehow causes a single helix structure to move from this mass, flip, and then thread itself together with another double helix structure.
When the atmospheric composition and sizes of these planets are accounted for, the planets can be assigned to several elements from the periodic table. Using this model, the planetary structure has been found to be an exact match to the genome of a human male, with 16% Irish heritage, 53% Indian heritage, 30.2% English descent and 0.2% from other locations. The individual is known to have blue eyes, red hair, and pale white skin. So far no exact genetic match for this has been found.

Date of Containment: 2014-05-12, After discovery by foundation FTL probe.
Location: Galaxy supercluster XY-539222.
Security Protocol: It is unlikely that UE-39221 will ever be discovered, due to extreme distances from the earth, and as such, no security protocol is needed.

All of these logs were pruned for length — entries to this list should be of relatively short length, and not extensively detailed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoy these logs but I feel like they need much more moderation to ensure relative consistency of writing style and also to ensure that they're all different.

Example: I feel like five or six of these are "normal location, normal stuff, but…. it's on tHe MoOn!!!!!1!"

item.title: Re: Question about Log Editing
item.content:encoded:
Am I allowed to edit logs?

Yes. Same edit rules as the rest of the mainsite; you can edit your own material freely (so long as it adheres to rules and isn't spamming/trolling, etc.), you make grammatical and mechanical corrections to others' work, major revisions and stylistic changes to others' work require permission given first.

Can I edit an old location I made to reword it, even if I can't prove I made it?

Hmm. If you can't prove that you made the entry and you're making major revisions, I'd get a staff member to sign off on the revisions. PM someone from the Rewrite Team and just send them a copy of the current entry and the revised version, and ask if you can make those changes. Remember to state the context of the request.

Or was the way locations are added changed? I was looking at some of the older versions from around the time I made my location and I saw that there was a "New post" option that took you to a separate page, rather than editing the page the old fashioned way. Is this why my post isn't appearing in history even though I made it?

I believe this is the case; previously the method that generated each entry with its own rating module was used. I think it turned out it actually was extremely messy and difficult to maintain, so it was changed back to the typical collab log format.

item.title: Question about Log Editing
item.content:encoded:
So I made Unexplained Location UE-867920 way back in June of 2017. It's the 5th one down from the top. I got back into SCP recently and of course looked at the old content I made. I saw that my location had some spelling errors and in general wasn't worded well, so I wanted to change it. Am I allowed to edit logs? This wouldn't be a problem if I could go to version history and show I made it, but for some reason I'm not in there. In fact it looks like a whole chunk from June 2017 to November 2018 is just missing. I'm talking about revision 130 to revision 131. Before and after that, there were and are multiple posts a month. Was there some kind of data corruption with the versions? Or was the way locations are added changed? I was looking at some of the older versions from around the time I made my location and I saw that there was a "New post" option that took you to a separate page, rather than editing the page the old fashioned way. Is this why my post isn't appearing in history even though I made it?

tl;dr Can I edit an old location I made to reword it, even if I can't prove I made it? Also, there is over a years worth of revisions straight up missing from the revision history.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - MAST - Open
item.content:encoded: Can we edit this unexplained events page and add our entries???

item.title: Staff Post - MAST - Open
item.content:encoded: Designations for individual logs have been reinstated from the log's former format.

item.title: Staff Post - MAST - Open
item.content:encoded:
The following entry was pruned from this page:

+ show block
– hide block

Description: A cube 10 m3 in volume. All humans who are caught in the sphere spontaneously vocalize, in English, "Man, I could sure go for a nice cold Coca Cola right now!" regardless of native language or knowledge of the Coca Cola brand.
Date of Containment: 1974-05-01
Location: In interplanetary space, static relative to the Sun at approximately the same altitude and inclination as Mars.
Security Protocol: After a similar location connected to a soda brand was found 6 years prior, modelling software capable of predicting future affected locations was developed. Currently, mentions of the above phrase are monitored on social media, with amnestics distributed at the discretion of security personnel.

This entry is almost identical to one of the most popular and oldest locations on the list, but replaces Pepsi for Coca-Cola, and Earth for Mars. Although it acknowledges the similarity and this was intentional, it still does not bring anything new to the table and has been removed for a lack of originality.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - MAST - Open
item.content:encoded:
All deleted entries — whether because they went below the -3 rating that this page used as a soft deletion threshold, or because they were pruned during the above update — can be found at this sandbox page.

Please contact pxdnbluesoul for any questions concerning the rating of any of these fragment pages before they were deleted.

item.title: Staff Post - MAST - Open
item.content:encoded:
This page has undergone a large-scale renovation. Two changes have been made:

  1. Firstly, the previously used format in which each entry had an individual rating module has been removed. Although a good idea in theory, the execution was complicated and not easily curated or maintained by either Tech Team or MAST. Going forward, this page will use the same format it initially used, which is the standard format for collab logs on the site, simply adding new entries to the bottom of the list.
  2. Secondly, a number of entries have been pruned, as they were decided to be not up to par of standards.

item.title: RIP me voting
item.content:encoded:
As said above, I get the Wikidot Account Module. While before it at least showed me signed in, now because of the Chrome thing… it doesn't.

*sighs*

item.title: Re: Admin and Technical Captain Post
item.content:encoded: No problem

item.title: Admin and Technical Captain Post
item.content:encoded:
This page is undergoing changes in the near future.

I have reverted your change, do not make further changes to this page.

item.title: Explaning my edit to this page
item.content:encoded: I have temporarily band-aid fixed the rating modules, it should still be looked at by a more experienced web developer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ^ Came here to say this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something is wrong with the rating modules on this page. Instead of the rating and buttons, it shows the Wikidot account module.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: yes we need one!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man,I sure do love being mentally deficient because I just messed the format all the way up.This is why I don’t write lmao

item.title: Re: Site Criticism Team - Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: okay

item.title: Site Criticism Team - Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
MikeLegacy6, don't reply to your own post. It's spammy and clogs up the discussion.

In the future, click 'Options' then 'Edit' on your initial post and you can then edit it to include any additional content.

item.title: Re: UE-951360, the River
item.content:encoded: Well, I guess removing it solved the problem.

item.title: UE-951360, the River
item.content:encoded: What is UE-951360's entry trying to say? I can see that "creators" is supposed to be "craters". As for the rest of it though, I'm confused. There are too many spelling and grammar errors for my mind to handle.

item.title: UE-875424 Does Not Belong
item.content:encoded:
http://www.scp-wiki.net/log-of-unexplained-locations/p/1#268

This newest one about the playground is an unexplained item, rather than location.

Also the short bit is bad writing ("truck-esque"?). It should read more like: "One of the climbing equipment changes from…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: New unexplained location.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: added antimemetic plastic

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My entry is literally just the state of Michigan, why do you dorks like it so much? :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can y'all stop writing full length SCPs for this thing? I swear some of these newer entries are longer than 173.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: These latest entries are just… really not good at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like my entry. I think I did a fine job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The show/hide messages aren’t showing up in the collapsibles, so I had to use bold text instead(big gun by dado entry). Is there a way to fix this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
“A sphere 5 meters in radius. All humans who are caught in the sphere spontaneously vocalize, in English, "Boy, I wish I had a nice cold Pepsi right now" regardless of native language or knowledge of the Pepsi brand.”

lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I put in a note but it didn't show up?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I just posted the longest entry on the entire log. I’m going to have bee nightmares tonight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. One YouTube video (I think it's a MostAmazingTop10 video) actually says that Sadako's Number is "the phone version of 'The Ring'."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No-one is actually interested in the dreams someone else had, unless they're being paid to analyse them.

No, dreams can make great story fuel.
For example:
https://www.bachelorsdegreeonline.com/blog/2010/15-famous-books-inspired-by-dreams

More locally, my own SCP-3519 is at +428 right now.

item.title: I have to wonder...
item.content:encoded:
Why would the foundation bother blowing up the ancient artefacts on Mars? It seems like a massive waste of resources, as well as a loss of research material, and you don't know what you might accidentally wake up. The very fact of a site being on Mars means it's pretty difficult for anyone else to run across by accident - the only visits or probes that will end up there over the next 50+ years will be quite large-scale organised efforts they could easily infiltrate and/or influence by other means to steer them away from the place and cover up anything that is still discovered anyhow. And remember what the foundation's initials expand to… it's not "seek and destroy".

On the other hand, surely that ice rink classifies as a scip in its own right, if the anomalous (and rather distressing) events continue to manifest? Shouldn't they have secured the site and be moving to try and rescue the people who appear below the ice, as well as figuring out where they're coming from and what's causing the teleportation in order to put a stop to it?

Rather like the pedestrian crossing one more than I probably should, though. It has echoes of a ghost story my grandad used to tell of regularly seeing a spectral cyclist on a certain stretch of road whilst riding the bus home of an evening. In both cases it's probably just a very peculiar trick of the light at a specific place, time and angle, but one that creates a convincing enough effect that multiple people end up seeing and investigating it, which then causes the foundation to take notice and, not being able to easily explain the events (maybe just through a lack of resources to have someone camp out at the sight for an entire year, seeing if they notice the same illusion, then going on to try and work it out from first principles), take more fundamental steps to prevent its recurrence and damp down any potential public hysteria…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Possibly if you convert the DMS numbers given into decimal degrees, they produce Pi? 8/60 is somewhere in the region of 0.14 after all… (now, where did I put my scientific calculator?)
that is all

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because people seem to think that giving achingly precise measurements makes a thing more sciencey and real seeming? Even though most real world scientific reports (other than those that genuinely use an electron microscope) don't tend to go much beyond 3 to 4 significant figures and often have considerable error bars, and can often show no figures at all, or just a roughly estimated range, for things we haven't been able to properly measure. Outer solar system astronomy, for example.

I thought the general thing with scips was to follow the X-Files rule? IE, if in doubt… obscure everything except the very bare bones needed to tell the rudiments of your story and then let your audience's imagination do the rest. Hence all the black boxes and [REDACTED]s. The numbers generally aren't even important, let alone their actual detail; much of the time, all you need is a certain number of blackout boxes suggesting a particular magnitude…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's probably the reason. No-one is actually interested in the dreams someone else had, unless they're being paid to analyse them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or in other words, they mashed up Tommy Tutone with The Ring?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don’t really get the entry about the area where a pie falls into the Gulf of Guinea. Apparently, someone does in-universe, but what exactly is the reason why it is at the coordinates 3° 8′ 29.7342″ N, 3° 8′ 29.7342″ E? I know that the ‘theme’ is pie/Pi Day/the number pi though.That one is kind of funny when you think about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to admit, that newest entry involving the Roblox death sound ("oof!") is much better than mine! Did you get the idea from me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The latest entry reminds me of a cursed phone number known as Sadako's number, which many people in Japan believe will cause you to die a week after calling it. If you call it, you will hear a weird noise. The number is 090████████.

item.title: Permalink?
item.content:encoded: Does anybody know if there is a way to link to an entry that isn't sensitive to its position on the list?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My most recent entry was based on a funny dream I had. Why did nobody like it?

item.title: Nothing to See Here, Move Along
item.content:encoded: UE-601616, a Doomsday Contest component.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd like to know how the hell they got some of these measurements to be so precise. Specifically for the ones that aren't a defined object or room, but just a range of effect. Like with the strawberry field one, did they have the subject scoot over 1 centimeter, stay there for 5 minutes, and then sniff them? They'd have to repeat that like 100 times. I do like that one, though.

When something is "exactly" 100 square meters or "exactly" 64 cubic kilometers, that makes more sense. No measurement is perfectly accurate, but if you measure 99.9968 square meters, it's probably 100 square meters, cause the even number is part of the spooky weird stuff, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i feel like the rating modules for all the entries break the suspension of disbelief.

item.title: Quick reminder
item.content:encoded: Make sure you follow standard tone and such when posting ANYWHERE in an article, aside from a -J. Just because these are short little things doesn't mean you can just crap then out with no effort.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Click on "Add New Item". It's right below the foreword.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How do I submit an unexplained location idea

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know if this is a browser issue on my end. But it seems like the rating models cut off the top half of words that pass under it. It's not that distracting but I feel like it could diminish peoples submission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Location Description: An apartment in Australia. When objects which are either identified as vacuum cleaners or function as a vacuum cleaner enter the apartment, they then experience gravity at roughly one-hundredth its usual strength. This effect does not reverse upon leaving the apartment. Identified following news reports showing a man "gliding using a Roomba".
Date of Containment: 2015-07-13
Location: Sydney, New South Wales
Security Protocol: The apartment has been bought by a Foundation proxy company and is now inhabited by an agent.

Wait, wouldn't the man still weigh the roomba down? How would he glide with it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Had an idea for a Scip: a haunted manor ride that was actually haunted (namely, the one in Disney World (I was just vacationing there)). Idea wasn't meaty enough for an article, so here it goes. Downvote for stupid SCP, bois.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean it could have just been bad luck but I'm not gonna go through the trouble if people didn't like it that much.

'Sides, it's just a UL.

I will say though I'm embarrassed about my mistake with the location, now. Waaay too specific.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://www.scp-wiki.net/log-of-unexplained-locations/rating/%3C=-3

Theirs is 390348, the "Chapter Barracks" game store. It is entry number 113 by the way.

The page with the rating is http://www.scp-wiki.net/log-of-unexplained-locations:113/norender/true and I suspect there is data in scpper about that, it just isn't set up to show invisible pages like this one.

I would PM Fifty-Nine or ask them in chat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there any way to see when this happened?

We might have info on voting dates (the scpper database site has a lot of data on votes) but I'm not sure if that applies for this sort of page.

Which entry was yours? Maybe people can discuss it here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So UL entries are automatically hidden after 3 down-votes?

Guess people weren't fans of mine then. Is there any way to see when this happened?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 to that. Anomalous items and events have wayy to many entries that are really below the site's standards, in concept and writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm loving the way the submission system works here. Could we maybe retrofit other collaborative pages to be like this?
